# COMP110 Students' Opnion on Pre-Lecture Videos

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="/prevideo_contribution_to_improve_performance/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Students' opinion on having optional pre-lecture videos

Students filled out a questionnaire that asked them various questions about who they were and some of their opinions about the class. When asked if pre-lecture videos would be helpful for their learning, many agreed. 

<img>

Students selected a number between 1, strongly disagree and 7, strongly agree. As shown, strongly agree was the most common answer.

<img>

When comparing students' preferance to having pre-lecture videos to their opinion on the difficulty of the class, those who selected 6 or 7 for pre lecture videos also answered higher when asked how difficult the class was for them. The question also ranged from 1 to 7. 1 signified they found the class to be very easy and 7 signified they found the class to be very difficult.

<img>

When comparing students' understanding of the clas to having pre-lecture videos, those who prefered pre lecture videos answered they have less understanding of the class. The question ranged from 1 to 7. 1 signified they did not understand anything and 7 signified they understand everything.


THe data suggests that many students are interested in pre-lecture videos, as shown by the distribution of responses. A large number of students selected higher values, which indicates agreement with the idea. The data also shows that students who report higher difficulty levels and lower understanding tend to show greater interest in pre-lecture videos. This suggests that pre-lecture videos could be especially helpful for students who are struggling with the course material. A potential downside of implementing pre-lecture videos is the requirement of additional time and effort from instructors to create and maintain these resources. Moreover, some students won't take advantage of them even if they are available. And yet, the potential benefits of improving student understanding and preparedness make this a valuable change to consider. In the future, this idea could be expanded by implementing pre-lecture videos and analyzing whether or not they improve student performance and confidence.
